title: Writing - Part 1: Biodiversity Loss
description: Your class has listened to a lecture on biodiversity loss and the mounting pressures that human activity places on the natural world and its ecosystems.

content.question: Write an essay discussing two of the challenges in your notes. You should explain which challenge you consider most urgent, giving reasons for your opinion.

opinions: Destroying habitats for farmland is the biggest driver of extinction.
Rising temperatures are disrupting ecosystems faster than species can adapt.
Overhunting and overfishing are pushing countless wild species to extinction.

structure.introduction: Introduce the biodiversity crisis and state which two challenges you will examine.
structure.bodyParagraph1: Analyse the first challenge, discussing its mechanisms and consequences for ecosystems.
structure.bodyParagraph2: Examine the second challenge and argue why you consider it the most urgent threat to biodiversity.
structure.conclusion: Summarise your analysis and reinforce which challenge demands the most immediate global response.

content.modelAnswer:
The accelerating loss of global biodiversity represents one of the most profound environmental crises of our time, with scientists warning that the current rate of species extinction is unprecedented in human history. Among the many pressures driving this decline, habitat destruction and climate change stand out as particularly consequential forces.

The conversion of natural habitats to farmland, urban development and infrastructure is widely acknowledged as the primary driver of biodiversity loss. When forests are cleared or wetlands drained, entire ecological communities are dismantled, not merely the species visible to the naked eye. The complex interdependencies between pollinators, plants, soil microorganisms and larger animals unravel rapidly once the habitat that sustains them is removed, often triggering cascading extinctions far beyond the immediate site of destruction.

Nevertheless, I would argue that climate change has now emerged as the most urgent challenge, precisely because of its globalscale reach. Unlike localised habitat destruction, climate change simultaneously alters temperature regimes, seasonal patterns and ocean chemistry across every biome on Earth. Species that might otherwise survive habitat pressures by dispersing to adjacent areas find that no refuge remains unaffected. Coral reef bleaching, Arctic sea-ice loss and the disruption of migratory timing illustrate how climate change attacks the foundations of ecosystems rather than individual components, and it operates on a trajectory that current mitigation efforts have so far failed to reverse.

In conclusion, while halting habitat destruction is indispensable, addressing climate change must be treated as the defining ecological priority of this generation.
